content.text: Every Claude initiative requires a fundamental decision: does the organization build the AI capability in-house, buy it from a vendor, partner with a platform provider, or combine these approaches? Making this decision ad hoc — based on what the CoE team knows how to build, or what a vendor is pitching — leads to inconsistent outcomes. A structured matrix anchors the decision in strategic logic.
The Two Dimensions That Drive the Decision
Two dimensions determine the right sourcing model for any Claude initiative:

does this capability directly differentiate the company in its market? A law firm's proprietary legal research AI is differentiating; its meeting transcription tool is not. Differentiation is not about the feature — it is about whether a better version of this AI gives the company a competitive edge that competitors cannot easily replicate.

how much ownership and control over the underlying model, data pipeline, and output logic does the company need? High-control requirements emerge from: regulatory obligations (the company must be able to audit exactly what the AI did), data sensitivity (training data cannot leave the company's environment), or long-term customization needs (the capability must evolve with proprietary data over years).

Most enterprises over-invest in building and under-invest in buying. The default assumption that 'building gives control' ignores the total cost of ownership: engineering time, maintenance, model updates, and opportunity cost.

Build in-house when the capability is a genuine source of competitive advantage and the organization needs deep control over the model, data, and decision logic. Building is the highest-investment path — it requires dedicated AI engineering, ongoing model maintenance, and a long development timeline. Reserve it for capabilities where the investment is justified by durable competitive value.
Proprietary underwriting models that embed years of loss data and actuarial judgment
Competitive pricing algorithms that incorporate internal cost structures and market positioning
Core R&D automation where the training data is the company's competitive moat
Customer lifetime value prediction models built on proprietary transaction history

When building, the Claude API is typically the foundation — not raw model training. 'Build' means building the application layer, prompt engineering, fine-tuning where needed, and the integration architecture — not training a model from scratch. Custom fine-tuning on Anthropic's platform, or deploying Claude on a private cloud, gives the control benefits of building without the cost of training from scratch.

Buy off-the-shelf SaaS AI when the capability is commodity and the organization does not need to own or customize the underlying logic. Buying maximizes time-to-value and minimizes ongoing maintenance burden. The trade-off is vendor dependency and limited customization.
Meeting transcription and summarization (Otter.ai, Fireflies, built-in Teams/Zoom AI)
Email drafting and writing assistants (Copilot for Microsoft 365, Grammarly Business)
Document processing and OCR with AI-extracted fields (various contract intelligence vendors)
IT helpdesk automation (ServiceNow AI, Zendesk AI)

The governance rule for bought AI: the CoE must still review the vendor's data handling practices, ensure the tool is in the approved vendor list, and confirm that the data flowing through the SaaS tool does not violate data residency or confidentiality requirements. 'We bought it from a reputable vendor' is not a sufficient governance check.
Partner / Integrate: Low Differentiation + High Control

When a capability is not differentiating but requires tight governance — often because of data sensitivity or regulatory requirements — the answer is to use a vendor API or platform integrated into the company's own infrastructure. This gives the compliance and audit benefits of control without the cost of building the AI layer.
Compliance monitoring using an established RegTech vendor's AI, integrated into internal workflows via API rather than SaaS
HR document summarization using Claude API deployed in the company's cloud environment — not consumer Claude.ai
Vendor-specific integrations where the vendor provides an AI SDK that the company deploys on its own infrastructure

The distinction from 'Buy' is where data goes: in the Buy quadrant, data goes to the vendor's cloud. In the Partner/Integrate quadrant, the AI runs in the company's environment (or a private cloud deployment) and data never leaves the company's control.
Build on Partner Platform: High Differentiation + Lower Control Tolerance

The fourth quadrant covers the most common enterprise Claude use case: building a high-value, differentiating application on top of the Claude API. The differentiation comes from the application logic, the prompt engineering, the data integration, and the user experience — not from training a custom model. The platform (Anthropic) provides the model; the company provides everything else.
Customer service AI that incorporates proprietary product knowledge, order history, and escalation logic
Sales intelligence built on CRM data and Claude's reasoning, producing custom competitor analysis and deal coaching
Internal knowledge search that retrieves from proprietary internal documents and synthesizes answers using Claude
Legal contract review that applies the company's specific clause library and risk tolerance to flagging issues

Building on a partner platform is not a compromise — it is the correct choice when your differentiation is in the application layer, not the model layer. Anthropic maintains the model; you own the product.
Applying the Matrix: A Decision Process
The matrix is most useful as a structured conversation tool, not a mechanical formula. Run this decision process for each new initiative in the CoE pipeline:

before scoring, write one sentence describing exactly what the AI does. Vague capability descriptions lead to vague sourcing decisions. 'AI for sales' is too broad; 'AI that generates first-draft outbound email sequences based on prospect industry and ICP match score' is scoreable.

ask: if a competitor had this exact capability with the same quality, would it materially affect our competitive position? High = yes, significantly. Low = no, or competitors already have equivalent capability.

ask: what are the consequences if we cannot audit, modify, or migrate away from this AI in 24 months? High = severe (regulatory risk, data lock-in, core product dependency). Low = manageable (we could switch vendors with 3 months notice).
use the matrix to identify the primary quadrant. For borderline cases, default to the lower-investment option and revisit after a 6-month pilot.

the sourcing decision has financial implications. A Build decision means internal headcount and multi-year investment. A Buy decision means a vendor contract and ongoing SaaS fees. Confirm that the sourcing model's cost profile aligns with the use case's approved budget.
Total Cost of Ownership by Sourcing Model
The sourcing decision is often made on up-front cost, which systematically underestimates the total cost of ownership (TCO) over a 3-year horizon. Use this TCO framework to compare options honestly:
These ranges are illustrative — actual costs depend heavily on use case complexity, team size, and vendor pricing. Use them as order-of-magnitude anchors, not budget commitments.
Several recurring patterns lead enterprises to the wrong sourcing decision:

internal tools like meeting summarization, writing assistance, or IT helpdesk drafting are commodity AI features. Building these in-house consumes engineering capacity better spent on differentiating use cases. If a vendor already does this at acceptable quality, buy it.

when a use case is genuinely differentiating, buying a vendor solution means competitors can buy the same solution. If an insurance company's claims triage AI is its primary competitive moat, buying it from a vendor that also serves all competitors erodes that moat.

highly sensitive data (PII, regulated records) requires high control but is not necessarily differentiating. HR systems handling employee PII need to run in the company's own environment, but the underlying AI capability is often commodity — the right answer is Partner/Integrate, not Build.
Ignoring switching costs in the Buy decision:

SaaS AI vendors can change pricing, deprecate features, or be acquired. For use cases that will become operationally embedded, evaluate how difficult it would be to switch vendors after 18 months of dependency. High switching cost in a non-differentiating tool is a governance risk.
The CoE should maintain a portfolio view of sourcing decisions, not just evaluate each use case in isolation. A healthy enterprise Claude portfolio typically shows:
Core differentiating applications; highest CoE investment
Productivity tools; fast deployment, low maintenance
Compliance or data-sensitive capabilities in own infrastructure
True competitive moat; only when justified by durable advantage

These percentages shift as the program matures. Early-stage programs lean more toward Buy (fast wins) and Build on Platform (core use cases). Mature programs have a more developed Buy portfolio running on autopilot and more Build on Platform use cases producing measurable business value.
Vendor Evaluation for the Buy and Partner Quadrants

Deciding to buy or partner is only the first step. Evaluating vendors in an AI context requires criteria beyond traditional software procurement because AI products have additional dimensions of risk: model quality, data handling, model updates, and output auditability.
Add these criteria to standard procurement scorecards when evaluating AI vendors:
Where does customer data go? Is it used to train the vendor's models? Can you opt out of training data usage?
Vendor cannot confirm data is not used for training; unclear data deletion policies
Can the vendor explain why the AI produced a specific output? Is there an audit log?
Black-box outputs with no reasoning trace; no audit capability
How often are models updated? Is there a testing window before updates go live? Can you pin to a specific model version?
Silent model updates; no rollback option; no advance notice of changes
What accuracy guarantees does the vendor offer? How are errors handled? Is there a compensation mechanism?
No accuracy commitments; vendor disclaims all responsibility for AI errors
Can you export your configurations, fine-tuning data, and audit logs if you leave? What is the exit timeline?
Data locked in proprietary formats; no export capability; 12+ month exit dependency
SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001, HIPAA BAA (if applicable), FedRAMP (if public sector)?
No third-party security certification; recent breaches not disclosed
Conducting an AI Vendor Proof of Concept
For any Buy or Partner decision above a threshold cost (suggested: $50K/year or operationally critical), require a structured proof of concept before signing a contract. A good AI vendor POC:
vendor demos using curated showcase data rarely reflect production performance. Insist on testing with representative samples of your actual data, anonymized where needed.
Tests failure modes, not just the golden path:

give the vendor's AI ambiguous inputs, edge cases, and adversarial examples. How does it handle inputs outside its training distribution? Does it fail gracefully (low confidence, escalate to human) or confidently wrong?

a POC evaluated only by the IT team and CoE will miss usability issues that matter to the people who will use it daily. Include 5–10 actual users in the POC and weight their satisfaction in the evaluation.
Measures against a defined success bar:
before the POC begins, define the minimum accuracy, throughput, and user satisfaction scores required to proceed. Post-hoc evaluation criteria are too easy to rationalize.
a POC that is not time-boxed tends to drift into a de facto pilot without a formal decision. Set a 4–8 week window and a decision date before the POC starts.
Build on Platform: Engineering Architecture Decisions

When the sourcing decision is 'Build on Claude API Platform,' the CoE engineering team faces a set of architectural choices that will shape the use case's quality, cost, and maintainability for years.

The two primary architectural patterns for Claude-based applications are direct prompt engineering and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG). Choosing between them is one of the first architectural decisions for any Build on Platform use case.
Reasoning over fixed context, analysis, drafting, transformation
Q&A over large knowledge bases, document retrieval, enterprise search
All relevant context fits in the prompt (< ~200K tokens)
Knowledge base too large for a single prompt; needs dynamic retrieval
Context injected at call time — always current
Retrieval index must be kept updated; stale index = stale answers
Lower — prompt design, API call, output parsing
Higher — embedding model, vector store, retrieval logic, re-ranking
Token cost scales with context length per call
Token cost lower per call (only retrieved chunks), plus vector store infra
High accuracy on provided context; limited to what you supply
Accuracy depends on retrieval quality; garbage in = garbage out

Most enterprise use cases start with direct prompt engineering because it is simpler to build and debug. RAG is introduced when the knowledge base exceeds what can fit in a prompt, or when retrieval latency and cost are acceptable trade-offs for broader coverage.
Prompt Engineering Patterns for Enterprise Reliability
Enterprise Claude applications require more prompt engineering discipline than a consumer-facing chatbot. Several patterns improve reliability at scale:

separate the system instructions (role, constraints, output format) from the user content (the actual document, question, or task). Changes to instructions should not require re-engineering the user content pipeline.

always specify the output format explicitly (JSON schema, markdown structure, numbered list) rather than relying on the model to infer it. Consistent format enables programmatic parsing and downstream processing.

instruct Claude to express uncertainty rather than fabricate. 'If you are not confident, say so and explain why' is more useful than the model producing a plausible-but-wrong answer with false confidence.

for complex reasoning (legal analysis, financial modeling, diagnostic reasoning), use a structured chain of thought: 'First identify the relevant facts. Then apply the relevant rule. Then state your conclusion.' This makes the reasoning auditable and improves accuracy.
treat prompts as versioned artifacts. When a prompt change improves accuracy for one case but degrades another, you need to know which version was used for each production output.

# Example: Enterprise prompt structure for document classification

SYSTEM:
You are a contract classification assistant for Acme Corp.
Your role is to classify contract documents into exactly one of these categories:
  - CUSTOMER: customer agreements, MSAs, SOWs with clients
  - VENDOR: supplier and vendor contracts
  - EMPLOYEE: employment agreements, offer letters, NDAs with employees
  - REGULATORY: regulatory submissions, government contracts
  - OTHER: anything that does not clearly fit the above

Output a valid JSON object matching this schema:
{
  "category": "<category name>",
  "confidence": "<HIGH|MEDIUM|LOW>",
  "reasoning": "<one sentence explaining the classification>",
  "flagged_for_review": <true|false>
}

Rules:
- If confidence is LOW or the document is ambiguous, set flagged_for_review to true
- Do not invent categories beyond the list above
- If the document content is insufficient to classify, output category: "OTHER"
  with confidence: "LOW" and flagged_for_review: true

USER:
Classify the following contract:

[document text here]
Enterprise Claude integrations must handle the reality that the Claude API is a network call with latency and rate limits. Design the integration pattern based on the use case's latency requirements:

user triggers the AI and waits for the response in the same session. Appropriate for interactive use cases: chat assistants, document Q&A, real-time analysis. Requires low-latency prompt design (avoid unnecessary context, use streaming) and graceful error display when the API is slow.

AI processing is queued and the result is delivered when ready. Appropriate for high-volume batch use cases: overnight contract review, bulk document classification, report generation. Enables much higher throughput and is resilient to API rate limits.

user triggers processing and receives a notification when it is complete. Appropriate for long-running tasks: comprehensive proposal generation, end-to-end research reports, multi-document synthesis. Best user experience for tasks that take 30 seconds to 5 minutes.

The sourcing model determines which governance controls the CoE must implement. A consistent governance framework that scales from Buy to Build avoids the pattern where different sourcing choices create governance gaps.

Governance is not a property of the technology — it is a property of the organization's decision to use it. A bought AI tool needs the same accuracy monitoring as a built one; the vendor does not own your governance obligation.
Sourcing decisions are not permanent. As a use case evolves — and as the Claude program matures — the right sourcing model may change. Common migration paths:
When a SaaS AI tool proves the value of a use case, but the organization outgrows the vendor's customization limits or decides it wants more control:
Trigger: vendor cannot support a required integration, accuracy is insufficient for the use case's actual data, or data residency requirements cannot be met by the vendor's SaaS model
Migration complexity: medium — the prompt engineering and integration work is net-new, but the use case is well-understood from the SaaS experience
Risk: the internal build will initially underperform the vendor's mature, fine-tuned product. Plan for a 3–6 month catch-up period
Data: ensure the SaaS contract allows export of usage logs and any customer-specific configurations before exit

When a Claude API application has been running for 12+ months, has generated a large labeled dataset from human review, and the organization decides the capability is core enough to justify fine-tuning or private deployment:
Trigger: accuracy needs that cannot be met by prompt engineering alone, latency requirements that the API cannot satisfy, or regulatory requirement that the model must run fully on-premises
Migration complexity: high — fine-tuning requires ML engineering capability, labeled training data (typically thousands of examples), and an evaluation framework
Cost: fine-tuning and private model hosting costs are typically 5–20x the Claude API cost at equivalent scale. Confirm that the accuracy or control benefit justifies the cost
Anthropic option: Claude model fine-tuning via Anthropic's fine-tuning API avoids building from scratch while still giving customized performance
Use this checklist when any new Claude initiative enters the CoE pipeline:
Written one-sentence description of exactly what the AI does
Competitive differentiation scored (High / Low) with justification
Strategic control requirement scored (High / Low) with justification
At least two alternative sourcing options considered and ruled out with written rationale
3-year TCO estimated for the chosen model
Vendor POC plan created (if Buy or Partner quadrant)
Governance controls mapped per sourcing model table
Exit / migration plan documented (what would trigger a sourcing model change?)
Finance sign-off on TCO and sourcing rationale

The Build-Buy-Partner matrix structures the sourcing decision for every Claude initiative using two dimensions: competitive differentiation and strategic control required. Build in-house for high-differentiation, high-control needs. Buy SaaS for commodity capabilities with low control requirements. Partner/Integrate for sensitive but non-differentiating capabilities. Build on the Claude API platform for the majority of high-value applications where differentiation is in the application layer. Apply total cost of ownership analysis to validate the decision financially, and maintain a portfolio-level view to ensure the sourcing mix aligns with the program's phase and strategic goals.
